title: Genus-protected higher-order topological phases
authors: Shahroze Shahab, Hui Liu, Daniel Varjas, Ion Cosma Fulga
abstract: Higher-order topological phases (HOTPs) feature protected gapless modes on boundaries of higher codimension, such as the corners or hinges of a crystal. They are understood as being protected by lattice symmetries: If the latter are broken, it becomes possible to remove the boundary modes without closing the bulk gap. In this work, we present construction schemes for HOTPs protected solely by the bulk gap, by fundamental symmetries, and by the global topology of the system shape (its genus, or number of holes), independent of any crystalline symmetries. As long as the fundamental local symmetries are preserved, the resulting boundary states cannot be removed by any purely-surface perturbation.
summary: This paper introduces a new class of higher-order topological phases protected by the global topology (genus) of a system rather than crystalline symmetries. It shows that in geometries like tori or Corbino disks, boundary modes are robust against surface perturbations. The work provides a complete topological classification for these phases in 2D and 3D.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether higher-order topological phases (HOTPs) can be protected by the global topology (genus) of a system's shape, rather than relying on specific crystalline symmetries.
main_result: The authors demonstrate the existence of 'genus-protected' topological phases (GPT phases) where boundary modes are protected by the bulk gap and the system's genus, and provide a topological classification using homology theory.
method: The study uses Volterra construction to introduce topological defects (dislocations and disclinations) and employs the Kwant package for numerical simulations of energy spectra, probability densities, and scattering-matrix invariants.
model_or_system: The research focuses on 2D and 3D systems, including the Benalcazar-Bernevig-Hughes (BBH) model on Corbino disks, Class-D Hamiltonians, and Class-DIII Hamiltonians on toroidal geometries.
key_observables: Majorana zero modes (MZMs), helical hinge/surface modes, energy spectra, real-space probability densities, and the reflection-matrix determinant (det(r)).
important_parameters: Genus (g), number of holes, bond dimerization (delta t), chemical potential (mu), and anisotropy parameter (alpha).
main_assumptions: The protection of boundary modes assumes the preservation of the bulk gap and fundamental local symmetries (e.g., time-reversal, particle-hole, or chiral symmetry).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the construction of higher-order topological phases, the effect of defects like disclinations, real-space probability densities of localized modes, and the topological classification for different genus surfaces.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic HOTPs, presents construction schemes for GPT phases using local and global defects, provides numerical verification via scattering invariants, and concludes with a general topological classification using homology theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. A cylinder consisting of an odd number of Kitaev chains (17 in this example). The 2D system is topologically equivalent to the Corbino disk of Fig. 1(b), having one hole (g = 1). There are two disjoint edges, each of which hosts an odd number of Majorana modes.

FIG. 1. Second-order states on different geometries. (a) On the disk, zero modes can be gapped out by bringing them together along the edges. (b) In a Corbino-disk geometry, the zero modes cannot be removed without closing the bulk gap, simply because they occur on two disjoint edges. (c) On the sphere, the 1D edge mode can be moved along the surface until it shrinks to a point (e.g. at the north pole) and gaps out. (d) In a torus-shaped 3D system, propagating modes on non-contractible loops cannot be removed without traversing the bulk.

FIG. 4. Probability density and low-energy spectrum near E = 0 of the Hamiltonian Eq. (1) discretized on a Corbino-disk geometry with local edge defects, as illustrated in Fig. 3(b), for a system of size (L, l) = (9.5, 4.5). In the real-space plots, darker color indicates a higher probability density. The insets show the energy spectrum E/t and the two red points correspond to the localized Majorana zero modes. (a) In the dimerized regime (δt = −0.5t), the system hosts two Majorana zero modes. (b) In the opposite regime (δt = +0.5t), two Majorana zero modes are again present, but they localize on the opposite pair of corners.

FIG. 3. Model of a 2D second-order topological superconduc- tor Eq. (1). Gray circles denote Majorana modes. (a) Real- space representation of the model Hamiltonian with hopping amplitudes t1,2 = t ± δt. Majoranas hopping along the red arrows acquire an additional π phase, resulting in a mag- netic flux threading each lattice plaquette. (b) Realization of a GPT phase by introducing local boundary defects on the square lattice with a square hole. Majorana modes located inside the red dashed rectangles are removed modifying the boundary geometry without affecting the bulk.

FIG. 5. BBH model with a non-uniform dimerization pattern. (a) The system hosts two MZMs in the topological regime, due to the presence of two local boundary defects (i.e. removing two sites). (b) Introducing a non-uniform dimerization pat- tern allows the realization of a trivial phase.
